Metric Glossary

Quick Reference
👁️
Impressions
The number of times an ad was displayed on screen. Counts every view, including repeat views to the same person — a measure of reach and exposure. e.g. 1.4M Meta impressions.
🖱️
Clicks
The number of times someone clicked an ad to visit the destination (link clicks). A direct signal of interest and intent to learn more. e.g. 21,275 Meta link clicks.
🎯
CTR — Click-Through Rate
Clicks ÷ impressions, shown as a percent. Measures how compelling an ad is at driving action, independent of how many people saw it. Education benchmark: 1.3–2.0% on Meta.
⏱️
Dwell Time
The average time a viewer spends with an ad in-feed (a LinkedIn engagement metric). Higher dwell time signals the content held attention and was actually consumed. e.g. 17.5s avg on LinkedIn.

KC Business Execs397,3201370.03%17.48s

✓ Chicago achieved highest dwell time at 17.87s. KS (Wichita) led on CTR at 0.04%. All audiences performing consistently with KU Brand benchmarks.
📉 Why impressions dipped from April (1.91M) to May (1.48M): This is a market reallocation, not a performance dip. Arizona — April's largest LinkedIn audience at ~775K — rotated off LinkedIn in May, and Kansas City was scaled back (715K → 397K). The new Chicago (457K) and Dallas (378K) alumni markets didn't fully replace that volume, and with total LinkedIn spend roughly flat, splitting it across more geographies lowers per-market impressions. Quality held throughout: dwell time stayed at 17.5s, with Chicago peaking at 17.87s.

Rollworks ABM Display

Account-Based Marketing

📋 Performance Summary

Rollworks ABM · May 2026 (full month)

The Rollworks ABM Display campaign targeted KU School of Business alumni contacts across three out-of-market cities. For the full month of May, the campaign delivered 30,124 total impressions and 10 clicks across contact targeting and account-based audiences — reaching employees at 611 unique accounts.

Contact targeting volume is now balanced across all three markets, with Chicago, Dallas, and Wichita each generating ~10,000 impressions. CTR for display ABM is typically low (0.02–0.08%) as the primary goal is awareness and account penetration, not direct click conversion.

The account reach is high-quality — ads appeared to employees at major financial services firms (Ameritrade, LPL, American Century, EY, KPMG), national professional services companies (IBM, Accenture, Robert Half, Deloitte, PwC), and regional anchors (Koch Industries, Burns & McDonnell, HNTB, Cerner/Oracle). This aligns well with the target "business executives and KU alumni" profile.

How ABM Targeting Works

Campaign Mechanics

Rollworks is an account-based marketing tool that serves digital ads to specific people at specific companies. Two different lists power the targeting, browser-cookie matching makes them ad-servable, and journey stages track how each company warms up over time.

📂The Two Lists

Account List — Who We Want to Reach

6,456 target companies

A curated set of organizations identified as good-fit accounts, built on company size, location, firmographic attributes, and education-specific exclusions. This is the universe of companies whose employees we want to influence — it defines where reach counts, and it's what journey stages are measured against.

Contact List — Who We Actually Serve Ads To

20,964 matched contacts

A list of individual people — in this case KU Alumni — uploaded as emails and matched to ad-servable cookies. The KU alumni list had an excellent match rate, producing a large, high-quality targetable audience. This is the "targeting list" the ads are delivered against.

🍪Contact-to-Cookie Matching
🔗
A contact list is just emails — ads can't be served to an email. Rollworks matches each email address to the browser cookies tied to that person's devices. Because one person browses on multiple devices and browsers, it's normal to match 3–10 cookies per email. That's why a contact list of 20,964 produces a much larger servable audience than 20,964 — and why impressions scale well above the raw list size.
🚦Journey Stages — How Companies Progress

Every account on the list is assigned a stage based on website activity. As a company's employees view more pages, the account moves up — letting us measure warming interest, not just ad delivery.

Unaware
No page views yet
Cold
→
Aware
At least 1 page view
Warming
→
Engaged
2+ page views
✓ 69 accounts
📈
As of May 31, 69 target accounts have progressed to Engaged (2+ page views) — including Bank of America, Charles Schwab, Fidelity Investments, Edward Jones, Raymond James, Burns & McDonnell, HNTB, Oracle, Microsoft, Workday, Thermo Fisher, and the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City. These are the warmest accounts in the funnel and the strongest signal that the right companies are paying attention.
🗺️How the Campaigns Are Structured
Now · May
  • One campaign per location — Chicago, Wichita, and Dallas.
  • Each targets the KU alumni contact list plus contextual keywords so ads appear on relevant content in that metro.
  • Balanced delivery at ~10,000 impressions per market.
Next · June
  • Budget doubles, split across two audience tracks.
  • Separate campaigns for Alumni and Business Executives.
  • Cleaner read on which audience drives engagement, plus expanded reach into the broader exec account list.
